name: Greek Folk Magic
description: Greek Folk Magic, also known as Hellenic Folk Magic, encompasses a diverse array of traditional beliefs and practices passed down through generations in Greece. It is deeply intertwined with the natural world, local spirits, and the veneration of both ancient Olympian deities and chthonic powers. Rooted in pre-Classical traditions and surviving through layers of cultural influence, including Byzantine Christianity, it involves charms, spells, rituals, and herbal remedies aimed at healing, protection, love, prosperity, and divination. Practices often focus on manipulating the energies of specific locations, such as springs, crossroads, and ancient ruins, and involve offerings and invocations to local spirits (djinns, nereids, and daemons) as well as the more prominent gods and goddesses. A central aspect is the belief in the evil eye (mati) and methods for averting or curing its effects, utilizing incantations, amulets, and specific gestures. The use of herbs and plants is extensive, with each plant possessing unique magical properties. Divination methods include scrying with water, olive oil, or coffee grounds. Greek Folk Magic emphasizes personal experience and adaptation, blending ancient beliefs with contemporary needs and concerns. It continues to thrive, particularly in rural areas, representing a living connection to Greece's rich cultural heritage and spiritual traditions. The strength of the magic is believed to come from the practitioner's 'meraki' - passion and love put into their work.
subtitle: Traditional magic of the Greek people

keyMyths.title: The Evil Eye (Mati)
keyMyths.description: The evil eye is a curse transmitted through a malevolent glare, often unintentional. Protection involves amulets, spitting, or uttering specific phrases to deflect the negative energy.

keyMyths.title: The Kallikantzaroi
keyMyths.description: Kallikantzaroi are goblins that emerge during the twelve days of Christmas, causing mischief. Protection involves burning specific herbs, hanging colanders, or leaving food offerings.

sources.author: Lawson, John Cuthbert
sources.work: Modern Greek Folklore and Ancient Greek Religion: A Study in Survivals
sources.description: A classic study examining the continuity of ancient religious elements in modern Greek folklore.
sources.text: A classic study examining the continuity of ancient religious elements in modern Greek folklore.

sources.author: Stewart, Charles
sources.work: Dreaming and Historical Trauma in Colonial Central Greece
sources.description: Explores the relationship between dreams, historical trauma, and folk practices in colonial Greece.
sources.text: Explores the relationship between dreams, historical trauma, and folk practices in colonial Greece.

sources.author: Danforth, Loring M.
sources.work: The Death Rituals of Rural Greece
sources.description: An ethnographic study of death rituals, including magical beliefs and practices associated with death and the afterlife.
